cards.text: The sons of Cush: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, and Sabteca.
cards.connection: Identifies the specific lineages that settled the Upper Nile and Horn of Africa.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the post-Babel settlement of Northeast Africa.

cards.text: Ah, land of whirring wings that is beyond the rivers of Cush...
cards.connection: A prophetic reference evoking the confluence of the Nile's tributaries where Cushite civilization flourished.
cards.cueContext: Used when describing the geographic heartland of Cush.

cards.text: ...from Migdol to Syene, as far as the border of Cush.
cards.connection: Defines the ancient southern boundary of Egypt, confirming the geographic divide between Mizraim and Cush.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing the border at the First Cataract.

cards.text: Now the king heard concerning Tirhakah king of Cush, 'Behold, he has set out to fight against you.'
cards.connection: Confirms the historical reality and geopolitical power of the 25th Dynasty Nubian pharaohs.
cards.cueContext: Used when discussing the Napatan confrontation with Assyria.

cards.text: And he rose and went. And there was an Ethiopian, a eunuch, a court official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who was in charge of all her treasure.
cards.connection: Demonstrates the reach of the Gospel into the royal court of Meroe and the historical reality of the Kandakes.
cards.cueContext: Shown when discussing Meroitic queens or the spread of early Christianity.

cards.text: Princes shall come out of Egypt; Cush shall hasten to stretch out her hands to God.
cards.connection: A prophetic promise fulfilled in part by the conversion of the Ethiopian eunuch and later the Aksumite empire.
cards.cueContext: Used to reflect on the spiritual destiny of the region.
